title: The connection between chronic sinus issues and suppressed crying
author: Kalesh
excerpt:

htmlContent:
I couldn't breathe. And not just because of allergies.

For years, I thought I just had bad sinuses. Seasonal allergies, maybe. A deviated septum. Dry air. I bought humidifiers, neti pots, allergy meds. I did the whole routine. And still, every few weeks, my sinuses would swell. My face would ache. I'd wake up with that pressure behind my eyes, that fog in my head that made it hard to think.

Here's the thing. I was also someone who didn't cry. Not really. Not in front of anyone. I had this belief, deep down, that crying was weak. That if I started, I wouldn't stop. That tears meant I was falling apart. So I held them in. I swallowed them. I tensed my throat and my jaw and my whole face, and I pushed the feeling down into my chest, where it sat like a stone.

And my sinuses kept getting worse.

It took me a long time to connect the two. I mean, really connect them. Not just intellectually, but in my body. The moment I realized that my sinuses might be holding tears I never let myself cry. That my face was a dam. That the pressure in my head was grief, not mucus.

Stay with me here. This isn't woo-woo. This is anatomy. This is physiology. This is what happens when your body has no other way to speak.

The anatomy of a held-back sob

When you suppress crying, you don't just hold back tears. You engage your entire upper body. Your throat constricts. Your jaw clenches. Your brow furrows. Your nasal passages narrow. You're literally squeezing shut the pathways that tears and mucus need to flow through.

Think about it. When you feel that urge to cry, and you stop it, where do you feel it? In your throat, right? That lump. That tightness. In your sinuses. Behind your eyes. In your chest. You're bracing against the wave. You're locking your body down.

Dr. Stephen Porges, the neuroscientist who developed Polyvagal Theory, talks about how our nervous system responds to threat. When we feel unsafe, we either mobilize (fight or flight) or immobilize (freeze or collapse). But here's the part that doesn't get talked about enough: we can also suppress. We can override our body's natural release mechanisms with sheer will. And that suppression has a cost.

Your sinuses are lined with mucous membranes that are directly connected to your tear ducts. When you cry, tears drain through your nasal passages. That's why your nose runs when you cry. It's all one system. So when you block crying, you're blocking a drainage system. And what happens when you block a drainage system? Pressure builds. Fluid gets stuck. Inflammation sets in.

Wild, right? Your sinuses aren't just for breathing. They're for crying.

I'm not saying every sinus infection is suppressed tears. I'm not saying allergies aren't real. But I am saying that if you have chronic, unexplained sinus issues, and you also have a history of not crying, of holding it in, of being the strong one, the stoic one, the one who never falls apart... then there might be a connection worth exploring.

What your body is saying when your sinuses won't clear

Dr. Gabor Mate, who has spent decades studying the link between stress and illness, writes about how emotional suppression shows up in the body. He talks about how our immune system, our endocrine system, our nervous system, they all talk to each other. And when you chronically suppress an emotion like grief, your body has to do something with that energy. It can't just disappear.

So it goes into your tissues. Into your fascia. Into your sinuses. Into your gut. Into your chronic pain. Into your fatigue.

Here's what I started to notice when I finally let myself cry. I mean really cry. Not a few tears. Not a choked sob. A full, ugly, heaving cry. The kind where you make sounds you didn't know you could make. The kind where your face is wet and your nose is running and you don't care who hears.

Afterward, my sinuses would clear. Completely. For hours. Sometimes for a whole day. The pressure would lift. I could breathe through both nostrils. My face felt light. My head felt clear.

And I thought: I've been treating the wrong thing.

I've been buying sprays and rinses and pills. I've been going to ENTs and getting scans. And what I actually needed was to cry. To grieve. To let the sadness move through me instead of getting stuck in my face.

Look, I'm not saying this is easy. It's not. If you've spent years, maybe decades, learning how not to cry, your body has built a fortress around that grief. Your throat has learned to lock. Your jaw has learned to clamp. Your sinuses have learned to swell shut. You can't just decide to cry and have it happen. It takes practice. It takes safety. It takes a nervous system that feels safe enough to let go.

A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored (paid link) by Deb Dana. She breaks down how to actually work with your vagus nerve, how to create safety in your body so that these release mechanisms can start to . It's not about forcing anything. It's about creating the conditions for your body to do what it naturally knows how to do.

The grief that lives in your face

There's a reason we say someone has a "heavy face" when they're sad. There's a reason we talk about "carrying grief" in our expressions. Your face holds emotion. Your sinuses are right there, in the middle of it all, part of the system.

Dr. Peter Levine, who developed Somatic Experiencing, talks about how trauma and unresolved emotion get stored in the body. He talks about pendulation, about completing incomplete responses. When you suppress crying, you're leaving a response incomplete. Your body prepared to release, and you stopped it. That incomplete response stays in your nervous system, in your tissues, waiting.

And your sinuses are part of that waiting.

I've worked with clients who had sinus surgery. Who had polyps removed. Who did every allergy treatment available. And still, the pressure came back. Still, the congestion returned. Because the root cause wasn't in their sinuses. It was in their history. In their grief. In the tears they never let themselves cry.

Something worth exploring for those drawn to body-based healing is In an Unspoken Voice (paid link) by Peter Levine. It walks through how the body holds trauma and how we can gently, safely start to release it. Not by force, but by attention.

I'm not saying you shouldn't see a doctor. Please see a doctor. Get your sinuses checked. Rule out structural issues. But also, ask yourself: When was the last time I really cried? When was the last time I let myself fall apart, even for a few minutes, in a safe place?

If the answer is "I can't remember" or "never," then you might have found a piece of the puzzle.

How to start letting the tears come

This isn't about forcing yourself to cry. That doesn't work. You can't bully your nervous system into releasing. What you can do is create conditions where crying becomes possible.

First, find a place where you feel safe. Really safe. Where no one will walk in. Where you won't be judged. Where you can make noise if you need to. This might be your bedroom. Your car. A shower. A therapist's office.

Second, bring your attention to your body. Notice your throat. Your jaw. Your sinuses. Just notice, without trying to change anything. You might feel a tightness, a pressure, a lump. That's the grief, waiting.

Third, breathe into those places. Not forcefully. Gently. Imagine your breath moving into your sinuses, into your throat, into your chest. Imagine it softening the edges of that held tension.

Fourth, if you feel the urge to cry, don't stop it. Don't judge it. Don't analyze it. Just let it happen. Let your face get wet. Let your nose run. Let sounds come out if they want to. Your body knows how to do this. It's been waiting.

A book that many trauma survivors find genuinely helpful is The Body Keeps the Score (paid link) by Bessel van der Kolk. It explains so clearly why our bodies hold onto what we can't express, and how we can start to release it. It's not an easy read, but it's an important one.

You might also find that having something grounding helps. A weighted blanket that many find calming for their nervous system is the YnM Weighted Blanket 15 lbs (paid link). The pressure can help your nervous system feel safe enough to let go.

I won't pretend this is a quick fix. It's not. If you've been holding back tears for years, your body has patterns. Deep patterns. It will take time to unwind them. You might cry and feel better for a day, then feel stuck again. That's normal. That's part of the process.

But here's what I know for sure: your sinuses might be trying to tell you something. Something that no spray, no pill, no surgery can fully address. They might be holding the tears you never let yourself cry. And the only way through is to let them fall.

Sit with that.

Notice what just happened in your body. Did your throat tighten? Did your sinuses ache? Did you feel a wave of something pass through your chest? That's your body talking. It's been talking all along. You just didn't know the language.

You're learning it now.
